The field has evolved from early studies on interface aesthetics and usability to sophisticated investigations using eye-tracking, fMRI, and physiological sensors to understand subconscious responses to visual stimuli. Recent scholarship emphasizes the use of visual cues to guide behavior, and the ethical implications of such interventions. The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated research on visual communication in remote work environments, while advances in AI-generated imagery have opened new questions about trust and authenticity in visual information processing.
INPUT (design)
Color, layout, visual encoding, interactivity
COGNITIVE PROCESS
Attention (salience-driven)
Perception (trust, satisfaction, loyalty)
Biases (anchoring, framing, overconfidence)
OUTPUT (behavior)
Risk perception
Decision Making
Existing studies:

Contribution:

Specific metric (UCR/DCR)

1. Website Design and Cognition
Deng, Liqiong, and Marshall Scott Poole. "Affect in Web Interfaces: A Study of the Impacts of Web Page Visual Complexity and Order." Mis Quarterly 34.4 (2010): 711-730. 
Main contribution: Developed a research model to explain the relationships among visual complexity and order design features of a webpage, induced emotional responses in users, and users’ approach behaviors toward the website as moderated by users’ metamotivational states.
Method: A laboratory experiment was conducted to test the model and its associated hypotheses. 
Key finding: A web user’s initial emotional responses (i.e., pleasantness and arousal), evoked by the visual complexity and order design features of a webpage when first encountered, will have carry-over effects on subsequent approach behavior toward the website. Webpage order has a negative relationship with the user's arousal, while webpage visual complexity bears a positive relationship with the user's arousal. Furthermore, there is a positive relationship between webpage order and web user's pleasantness in the telic condition and a positive relationship between webpage visual complexity and user's pleasantness in the paratelic condition. Users' metamotivational states have significant moderation effects on their feeling of pleasantness and subsequent approach tendency toward the website.
Relevance: Web visual order, emotional response, approach behaviour
Hong, W., Thong, J. Y., & Tam, K. Y. (2004). Does animation attract online users’ attention? The effects of flash on information search performance and perceptions. Information Systems Research, 15(1), 60-86.
Main contribution: Focusing on flash animation, this study examines its effects on online users’ performance and perceptions in both task-relevant and task-irrelevant information search contexts.
Method: laboratory experiment 
Key finding: In the task-relevant context, flash is applied on the search target; while in the task-irrelevant context, flash is applied on a nontarget item. The results of this study confirm that flash does attract users’ attention and facilitates quicker location of the flashed target item in tightly packed screen displays. However, there is no evidence that attracting attention increases recall of the flashed item, as is generally presumed in practice, and may even decrease the overall recall. One explanation is that when users have to use their limited attentional resources on suppressing the distraction of flash, they will have less mental resources to process information. Moreover, the results suggest that processing information about an item depends not only on the attention it attracts per se, but also on the attention that other items on the same screen attract. While flashing an item may not increase the recall of that item, it can reduce the recall of other items (especially the nontarget items) on the screen. Finally, flash has negative effects on users’ focused attention and attitude towards using the website. 
Relevance: Web information, visual search, limited attention, cognitive psychology
Kim, Jinwoo, Jungpil Hahn, and Hyoungmee Hahn. "How do we understand a system with (so) many diagrams? Cognitive integration processes in diagrammatic reasoning." Information Systems Research 11.3 (2000): 284-303.
Main contribution: This study proposes a theoretical framework that focuses on the cognitive processes of perceptual and conceptual integration.
Key finding: Understanding a system represented by multiple diagrams involves a process of searching for related information and of developing hypotheses about the target system. The results also showed that these perceptual and conceptual integration processes were facilitated by incorporating visual cues and contextual information in the multiple diagrams as representation aids. Visual cues indicate which elements in a diagram are related to elements in other diagrams; the contextual information indicates how the individual datum in one diagram is related to the overall hypothesis about the entire system. 
Relevance: Visual cues, cognitive integration
Cyr, Dianne. "Modeling web site design across cultures: relationships to trust, satisfaction, and e-loyalty." Journal of management information systems 24.4 (2008): 47-72.
Main contribution: Model the relationship of Web site design to trust, satisfaction, and loyalty across cultures. 
Method: Utilizing data collected from 571 participants in Canada, Germany, and China, various relationships in the research model are tested using partial least squares analysis for each country separately. 
Key finding: Differences are determined for separate country samples concerning whether navigation design, visual design (overall graphical look, includes colors, photographs, shapes, or font), and information design result in trust, satisfaction, and ultimately loyalty-suggesting design characteristics should be a central consideration in Web site design across cultures.
Relevance: Website visual design, trust and satisfaction
Speier, Cheri, and Michael G. Morris. "The influence of query interface design on decision-making performance1." MIS quarterly 27.3 (2003): 397-423.
Main contribution: Compared the use of visual and text-based query interfaces on both low and high complexity tasks.
Method: Laboratory experiments  
Key finding: Decision maker performance was more accurate using the text-based interface when task complexity was low; however, decision makers using the visual interface performed better when task complexity was high. In addition, decision makers’ subjective mental workload was significantly lower when using the visual interface, regardless of task complexity. In contrast to expectations, less time was needed to make a decision on low complexity tasks when using the visual interface, but those results were reversed under conditions of high task complexity.
Relevance: Website visual design, decision-making, task complexity
Jiang, Zhenhui, et al. "The determinants and impacts of aesthetics in users’ first interaction with websites." Journal of Management Information Systems 33.1 (2016): 229-259.
Main contribution: Synthesize prior literature from different academic domains and propose users’ perceived quality of five design elements (i.e., unity, complexity, intensity, novelty, and interactivity) as determinants of website aesthetics. They further theorize the effects of aesthetics on users’ attitudes toward the website and their perception of the corporate image.
Method: Two studies (card sorting and a survey) were conducted to test the research model. 
Key finding: Confirm the determinants of website aesthetics and find that users’ perception of aesthetics has significant impacts on perceived utility and their attitudes toward the website, which further affects the corporate image exhibited via the website. In addition, users’ first interaction with a website, perceived aesthetics has a larger impact on their attitudes toward the website than perceived utility.
Relevance: Website visual design, cognitive anchoring
Hong, Weiyin, James YL Thong, and Kar Yan Tam. "The effects of information format and shopping task on consumers' online shopping behavior: A cognitive fit perspective." Journal of management information systems 21.3 (2004): 149-184.
Main contribution: Developed a research model to investigate the cognitive fit between information format and shopping task, and examine its influence on consumers' online shopping performance and perceptions of shopping experience.
Method: An experiment was conducted to examine the effects of two types of information formats (list versus matrix) in the context of two types of shopping tasks (searching versus browsing). 
Key finding: When there is a match between the information format and the shopping task, consumers can search the information space more efficiently and have better recall of product information. Specifically, the list format better supports browsing tasks, and the matrix format facilitates searching tasks. However, a match between the information format and the shopping task has no effect on cognitive effort or attitude toward using the Web site. 
Relevance: Website information format, cognitive fit theory, competition for attention theory
Lee, Younghwa, and Andrew NK Chen. "Usability design and psychological ownership of a virtual world." Journal of Management Information Systems 28.3 (2011): 269-308.
Main contribution: Apply concepts of Web site usability and psychological ownership to develop a model that illustrates the relationships between seven usability factors (legibility, firmness, coherence, variety, mystery, classic, and expressive visual aesthetics) of a virtual world, four antecedents of psychological ownership (cognitive appraisals, perceived control, affective appraisals, and self-investment).
Method: A cross-sectional study with 239 Second Life users
Key finding: Designing a usable virtual world that induces strong psychological ownership is crucial to attract users to spend more time, participate in more activities, and revisit the virtual world. Designers can benchmark and evaluate the usability of their current virtual worlds, compare the results to the designs of competitors, and upgrade the offerings of virtual worlds, as needed, by allocating available resources to the most influential design factors to suit their specific needs.
Relevance: Website information format, cognitive fit theory, competition for attention theory
Minas, R. K., & Dennis, A. R. (2019). Visual background music: creativity support systems with priming. Journal of management information systems, 36(1), 230-258.
Main contribution: They conducted two experiments testing an approach that builds on priming research by providing stimuli related to achievement on the screen as users worked, called visual background music. 
Key finding: This approach increased an individual’s creative output by 10–40% depending upon the experiment and outcome measure. Under the conditions of their studies, the priming stimuli they used had the strongest effect on the flexibility pathway by broadening the user’s consideration of the solution space, rather than the persistence pathway of encouraging more ideas within a topic category. 
Relevance: Visual information format, priming creativity
Nadkarni, Sucheta, and Reetika Gupta. "A Task-Based Model of Perceived Website Complexity." MIS quarterly 31.3 (2007): 501-524.
Main contribution: They propose that perceived website complexity (PWC) is central to understanding how sophisticated features of a website (such as animation, audio, video, and rollover effects) affect a visitor’s experience at the site.
Method: They draw on the task complexity literature to develop a broad and holistic model that examines the antecedents and consequences of PWC. 
Key finding: First, the positive relationship between objective complexity and PWC was moderated by user familiarity. Second, the relationship between PWC and user satisfaction was negative for goal-directed users and inverted-U for experiential users. 
Relevance: Website information, user perception
Hu, Paul Jen-Hwa, Han-fen Hu, and Xiao Fang. "Examining the Mediating Roles of Cognitive Load and Performance Outcomes in User Satisfaction with a Website: A Field Quasi-Experiment." MIS quarterly 41.3 (2017): 975-987.
Main contribution: To extend understanding of the drivers of user satisfaction with a website, this study focuses on the mediating roles of cognitive load and performance outcomes (in the form of efficiency and accuracy) according to a cognitive cost–benefit framework and cognitive load theory.
Method: The hypotheses were tested with data from a field quasi-experiment among 238 participants using two websites.
Key finding: Both navigation structure design and user familiarity can increase satisfaction by enhancing performance outcomes and reducing cognitive load. Cognitive load appears to affect user satisfaction more than performance outcomes do, in that people seem to recognize the cognitive processing they undertake and have a good sense of the time and physical effort needed to perform a task. Efficiency and accuracy also seem to have differential effects on user satisfaction, constituting two fundamental, distinct dimensions of performance outcome.
Relevance: Website navigation structure design, user familiarity, cognitive load, performance outcomes
Cyr, Dianne, et al. "Exploring Human Images in Website Design: A Multi-Method Approach." MIS quarterly 33.3 (2009): 539-566.
Main contribution: how Internet users perceive human images as one element of website design
Method: A controlled experiment was conducted using a questionnaire, interviews, and eye-tracking methodology. Three conditions of human images were created including human images with facial features, human images without facial features, and a control condition with no human images
Key finding: Human images with facial features would induce a user's emotive reaction in which the website would appear more appealing, have warmth or social presence. No direct relationship was found between the human image conditions and trust. Additional analyses revealed subtle differences in the perception of human images across cultures (Canada, Germany, and Japan). While the general impact of human images seems universal across country groups, based on interview data four concepts emerged—aesthetics, symbolism, affective property, and functional property—with participants from each culture focusing on different concepts as applied to website design.
Relevance: Website information, human image, trust, culture
Reinecke, Katharina, and Abraham Bernstein. "Knowing What a User Likes: A Design Science Approach to Interfaces that Automatically Adapt to Culture." Mis Quarterly 37.2 (2013): 427-453.
Main contribution: They argue that a more comprehensive personalization of interfaces to cultural background is needed to appeal to users in expanding markets.
Key finding: This paper introduces a low-cost, yet efficient method to achieve this goal: cultural adaptivity. Culturally adaptive interfaces are able to adapt their look and feel to suit visual preferences. They evaluate the efficacy of the prototype’s automatically generated interfaces by comparing them with the preferred interfaces of 105 Rwandan, Swiss, Thai, and multicultural users. The findings demonstrate the feasibility of providing users with interfaces that correspond to their cultural preferences in a novel yet effective manner.
Relevance: Visual preferences, cultural adaptivity
Zhang, Cheng, Peijian Song, and Kai Lim. "Standardize or let a thousand flowers bloom? Interface design coordination between software platforms and hosted apps." MIS quarterly 47.3 (2023): 1333-1353.
Main contribution: Drawing on the theory of basic Gestalts, they theorize the three interface design properties of interface similarity, embeddedness, and synchrony, and extend the theory to investigate how these properties help both apps and their host platform attract each other’s users, thus reflecting derivative usage between them. 
Method: They performed a randomized field experiment to test the proposed hypotheses.
Key finding: Interface similarity, embeddedness, and synchrony of the app-platform interface design strengthen users’ grouping perception of the platform and its apps, thus enhancing platform-to-app forward derivative usage. This strengthened grouping perception does not improve the app-to-platform backward derivative usage.
Relevance: Website interface design, visual perception
Adipat, Boonlit, Dongsong Zhang, and Lina Zhou. "The Effects of Tree-View Based Presentation Adaptation on Mobile Web Browsing." Mis Quarterly 35.1 (2011): 99-121.
Main contribution: Based on cognitive fit theory and information foraging theory, they propose a novel hybrid approach to adapting Web page presentation that integrates three types of adaptation techniques, namely tree-view, hierarchical text summarization, and colored keyword highlighting.
Method: By following the design science research framework, they implemented the proposed approach on handheld devices and empirically evaluated the effects of presentation adaptation on mobile Web browsing. 
Key finding: First, the tree-view based presentation adaptation can significantly reduce the search time and increase the accuracy of information search via Web browsing on a handheld device, as well as increase perceived ease of use and usefulness of mobile system. Second, hierarchical text summarization provided on tree view did not have the significant impact on user performance in information search and perception of mobile systems. Third, providing colored keyword highlighting in tree view reduced search time by enabling users to perform effective scanning/skimming. The positive impact of colored keyword highlighting on PEOU and PU of mobile Web systems was moderated by task complexity, which was manifested by its stronger effect for complex search tasks than for simple. Fourth, the results show that the effects of presentation adaptations are generally greater for complex tasks than for simple task. Fifth, providing additional presentation adaptation (e.g.,colored keyword highlighting) to the tree view could further improve users' information search performance and perception.
Relevance: Website interface design, cognitive fit theory, information foraging theory, colored keyword highlighting
Wells, John D., Joseph S. Valacich, and Traci J. Hess. "What Signal Are You Sending? How Website Quality Influences Perceptions of Product Quality and Purchase Intentions." MIS quarterly 35.2 (2011): 373-A18.
Main contribution: This research proposes a model to investigate website quality as a potential signal of product quality and consider the moderating effects of product information asymmetries and signal credibility.
Method: Three experiments are reported that examine the efficacy of signaling theory as a basis for predicting online consumer behavior with an experience good.
Key finding: Website quality influences consumers’ perceptions of product quality, which subsequently affects online purchase intentions. Additionally, website quality (conceptualized as a second-order formative construct formed by the four, first-order dimensions of security, download delay, navigability, and visual appeal) was found to have a greater influence on perceived product quality when consumers had higher information asymmetries. Likewise, signal credibility was found to strengthen the relationship between website quality and product quality perceptions for a high-quality website. 
Relevance: Signaling theory, quality information, information asymmetries, decision-making
Benbasat, Izak, and Albert S. Dexter. "An experimental evaluation of graphical and color-enhanced information presentation." Management science 31.11 (1985): 1348-1364.
Main contribution: A laboratory experiment was conducted to assess the influence of graphical and color-enhanced information presentation on decision quality, decision-making time and user perceptions of information systems attributes.
Method: The experimental design examined the main and interaction effects of report format, color, and individual differences (field dependence/independence) among the subjects. 
Key finding: The claims made about the benefits of color-enhanced reports are subject to qualification. Even though color influenced decision-making quality in general, and to some extent color was more beneficial for graphical than tabular reports, its most significant impact was on the performance of field-dependents. The decision-making quality of field-dependents with color-enhanced reports was 73% better than field-dependents who did not have such reports. There were no performance differences between subjects who used tabular and graphical reports. This outcome is explained by taking into consideration the underlying nature of the task given to the subjects and how the reports were organized to support this task. 
Relevance: Color-enhanced information, decision-making, user perception
Li, Hanwei, et al. "Estimating and exploiting the impact of photo layout: A structural approach." Management Science 69.9 (2023): 5209-5233.
Main contribution: Host-generated property images as a visual channel reveal substantial information about properties. They define, estimate, and optimize the impacts of Airbnb photos on customers’ renting decisions. 
Method: They apply ResNet-50, a convolutional neural network model, to build two separate, supervised learning models to evaluate the image quality and room types posted by Airbnb hosts. Then, they characterize the overall impacts of photo layout by the room type featured in the photo, photo quality, and order of display on the listings’ web pages. To address two estimation challenges in the Airbnb setting, namely, censored demand and changing consideration sets, they propose a novel pairwise comparison model that utilizes customers’ booking sequence data to consistently estimate the impact of photo layout on customers’ renting decisions.
Key finding: The cover image has a significantly larger impact than noncover photos and a high-quality bedroom cover image leads to the largest increase in demand. Furthermore, they build a nonlinear integer programming optimization problem and develop an algorithm to determine the optimal photo layout. The counterfactual analysis suggests that a listing’s unilateral adoption of optimal photo layout leads to 11.0% more bookings on average. Moreover, depending on the neighborhood and market size, when listings simultaneously switch to the optimal photo layout, they get booked for two to five additional days in a year on average, which boosts revenue by $500 to $1,100.
Relevance: Visual information, photo-layout, decision-making, user perception
Pieters, Rik, Michel Wedel, and Jie Zhang. "Optimal feature advertising design under competitive clutter." Management Science 53.11 (2007): 1815-1828.
Main contribution: This study investigates consumers' attention to retail feature ads and proposes a method to optimize the design of the ads.
Method: Utilizing a large dataset of consumers' attention to over 1,100 individual feature ads collected with eye-tracking technology, they analyze the effects of the five key design elements of feature ads—brand, text, pictorial, price, and promotion—on consumers' attention to them. Attention is measured in terms of selection and gaze duration. They focus on the effects of the surface sizes of the design elements. A key feature of the model is that it takes into account the impact of visual clutter in the ad display page. To capture the clutter effects, they propose two new entropy-based measures that characterize the salience of feature ads in their competitive environment based on Attention Engagement Theory. In a Bayesian framework, they simultaneously estimate the parameters of the model and optimize the design of feature ads in terms of surface sizes of the five design elements.
Key finding: Significant improvements in attention to feature advertising can be achieved without increase in costs. The optimization results indicate that,overall, the pictorial and text elements in feature ads should be reduced in size, whereas the sizes of the other three elements should be enlarged.
Relevance: Visual elements sizes, attention engaged theory
Cyr, Dianne. "Modeling web site design across cultures: relationships to trust, satisfaction, and e-loyalty." Journal of management information systems 24.4 (2008): 47-72.
Differences are determined for separate country samples concerning whether navigation design, visual design, and information design result in trust, satisfaction, and ultimately loyalty.
Lim, Kai H., and Izak Benbasat. "The Effect of Multimedia on Perceived Equivocality and Perceived Usefulness of Information Systems." MIS quarterly 24.3 (2000): 449-461.
They develop a task-representation fit model to generate several predictions about the potential of multimedia  (text, graphics, audio, video, and animations) to alleviate the limitations of text-based information in the context of individual decision makers utilizing organizational data and test them in a laboratory experiment. Results support the task-representation fit relationships predicted. For analyzable tasks, text-based representation and multimedia representation are equally effective in reducing perceived equivocality levels. For less-analyzable tasks, only multimedia representation was instrumental in reducing perceived equivocality levels.
Relevance: Information presentation, task analysability
Ye, Xuhong, et al. "Developing and testing a theoretical path model of web page impression formation and its consequence." Information Systems Research 31.3 (2020): 929-949.
Main contribution: Drawing on impression formation literature and cue utilization theory, they formulate a theoretical model of web page impression formation with a temporal sequence of processing stages: automatic processing, initial perception confirmation, and impression formation, which in turn affects approach-avoidance tendency.
Method: They conduct two within-subject studies to collect both self-reported and eye movement data to test predictions. 
Key finding: Using real-world web pages as stimuli, the first study reveals that people engage in both automatic processing and attentive processing to form web page impressions, and arousal (measured by pupil size) shapes how people allocate attention (measured by peak duration and fixation count) to visual aesthetics. Using web pages with manipulated aesthetics in the second study, they illuminate the theoretical logic of web page impression formation by showing that people first engage in automatic processing and then engage in attentive processing. Both studies provide strong empirical support for the mediating role of arousal on the effects of visual aesthetics on attention allocation. 
Relevance: Web visual aesthetics, automatic processing, attentive processing, arousal 
Lee, Younghwa, Andrew NK Chen, and Virginia Ilie. "Can Online Wait Be Managed? The Effect of Filler Interfaces and Presentation Modes on Perceived Waiting Time Online1." Mis Quarterly 36.2 (2012): 365-394.
Main contribution: By adopting resource allocation theory, cognitive absorption theory, and human computer interaction (HCI) theories (competition for attention, visual search, and motion effect), they design diverse filler interfaces and investigate their effects on antecedents of perceived waiting time (PWT). 
Method: The proposed research model considers cognitive absorption factors such as temporal dissociation, focused immersion, and heightened enjoyment as antecedents of PWT, which in turn triggers three outcomes: affective appraisals, cognitive appraisals, and Web site use intention. Recent scholarship emphasizes the use of visual cues to guide behavior, and the ethical implications of such interventions. The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated research on visual communication in remote work environments, while advances in AI-generated imagery have opened new questions about trust and authenticity in visual information processing.

    22→Color, layout, visual encoding, interactivity
