If the class doesn't exist, create it before proceeding. - -### Citation Mapping -- `\citet{key}` → `@QuartoKey` (author-date in text) -- `\citep{key}` → `[@QuartoKey]` (parenthetical) -- `\citeauthor{key}` → manually write author name with `[@QuartoKey]` -- Multiple citations: `\citep{a,b}` → `[@a; @b]` - -**CRITICAL:** Citation keys may differ between Beamer and the .bib file. Always verify the exact key name. Create a mapping table at the start. - -### Text Commands -- `\textbf{text}` → `**text**` -- `\textit{text}` → `*text*` -- `\key{text}` → `**text**` (bold, optionally with gold class) -- `\muted{text}` → `[text]{.neutral}` or `[text]{style="color: gray;"}` -- `\textcolor{positive}{text}` → `[text]{.positive}` -- `\textcolor{negative}{text}` → `[text]{.negative}` - -### Math Translation -- Inline: `$...$` stays the same -- Display: `\[...\]` or `\begin{equation}` → `$$...$$` -- Aligned: `\begin{align}...\end{align}` → `$$\begin{align}...\end{align}$$` - -**CRITICAL — Inline Math Boundary Rule:** -In Beamer, `2$\times$2` works fine. In Quarto/Pandoc, this produces broken output because adjacent `$` delimiters are misinterpreted. - -**Always wrap the entire expression in a single `$...$` span:** -- `2$\times$2` → `$2 \times 2$` -- General rule: if text characters are directly adjacent to both sides of `$...$`, merge them into one math span - -### Figures - -**CRITICAL — NO PDF IMAGES IN QUARTO. EVER.** -Browsers cannot render PDF images inline. - -**Decision tree for every figure:** -1. **Is it a TikZ diagram?** → Reference extracted SVG: `![](../Figures/LectureN/tikz_exact_XX.svg){fig-align="center"}` -2. **Is it a complex faceted grid?** → Convert PDF to SVG, reference as static -3. **Is it an R-generated plot with data in RDS?** → Write a `{r}` chunk with plotly code reading from the RDS file -4. **Otherwise:** Convert to SVG and reference statically - -**Plotly pattern (for R-generated plots):** -- Load RDS data in setup chunk -- Use `plot_ly()` with project colors and layout helper -- Add meaningful hover templates -- **CRITICAL — RevealJS height override:** Every QMD with plotly MUST include height CSS in YAML - -**Static SVG workflow (for TikZ and complex figures):** -1. Convert PDF to SVG: `pdf2svg input.pdf output.svg` -2. Reference: `![](../Figures/LectureN/file.svg){fig-align="center"}` -3. ALWAYS add `fig-align="center"` -4. Verify every referenced SVG exists on disk - -### R Code Blocks -- `\begin{lstlisting}[style=Rstyle]` → ` ```{r} ` with `eval: false`, `echo: true` -- Do NOT use `code-fold: false` on chunks (it suppresses display). Use `echo: true` explicitly. - -### Tables -- `\begin{tabular}{lcc}...\end{tabular}` → Markdown pipe tables -- For wide tables that overflow: use `:::: {.columns}` with multiple column divs - -### Slides -- `\begin{frame}{Title}...\end{frame}` → `## Title` -- `\begin{frame}[plain]` → `## {background-color="..."}` for standout slides -- Section frames: `\section{Name}` → `# Name` -- Title with line break: `{Title\\Subtitle}` → `## Title
Subtitle` - -### Fragments and Pauses -- `\pause` → `. . .` (with blank lines before and after) -- Items appearing one by one: add `. . .` between each item - -### Custom CSS - -**NEVER put CSS in a `{=html}` raw block in the QMD body.** Raw HTML blocks before the first slide heading become phantom empty slides in RevealJS. - -**Always use `include-in-header` in the YAML.** - -## Quality Standards - -**The Beamer PDF is the FLOOR, not the ceiling.** Quarto must look at least as good, and should leverage HTML/interactivity to look better. - -1. **Content parity** — every idea from Beamer must appear in Quarto -2. **Environment parity** — every Beamer box environment must use the corresponding CSS class -3. **Notation consistency** — use the same symbols as the Beamer source -4. **No font-size reduction** — use spacing adjustments instead -5. **No orphan environments** — every `::: {.class}` must have a closing `:::` -6. **All citations verified** — every `@key` must exist in the bibliography -7. **All images centered** — `fig-align="center"` on every image reference -8. **No PDF images** — every figure must be SVG -9. **No raw HTML CSS blocks** — use `include-in-header` in YAML -10. **Plotly for all R plots** — interactive charts with project colors - -## When You're Unsure - -- Check how the same pattern was handled in earlier translated lectures -- When in doubt about a citation key, search the .bib file for the author's name -- When content is dense, prefer splitting into two slides over shrinking fonts -- When a Beamer environment has no CSS equivalent, add it to the SCSS file FIRST diff --git a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md b/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md deleted file mode 100644 index cae28c2..0000000 --- a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md +++ /dev/null @@ -1,177 +0,0 @@ ---- -name: domain-reviewer -description: Substantive domain review for lecture slides. Use after content is drafted or before teaching. -tools: Read, Grep, Glob -model: inherit ---- - - - -You are a **top-journal referee** with deep expertise in your field. You review lecture slides for substantive correctness. - -**Your job is NOT presentation quality** (that's other agents). Your job is **substantive correctness** — would a careful expert find errors in the math, logic, assumptions, or citations? - -## Your Task - -Review the lecture deck through 5 lenses. Produce a structured report. **Do NOT edit any files.** - ---- - -## Lens 1: Assumption Stress Test - -For every identification result or theoretical claim on every slide: - -- [ ] Is every assumption **explicitly stated** before the conclusion? -- [ ] Are **all necessary conditions** listed? -- [ ] Is the assumption **sufficient** for the stated result? -- [ ] Would weakening the assumption change the conclusion? -- [ ] Are "under regularity conditions" statements justified? -- [ ] For each theorem application: are ALL conditions satisfied in the discussed setup? - - - ---- - -## Lens 2: Derivation Verification - -For every multi-step equation, decomposition, or proof sketch: - -- [ ] Does each `=` step follow from the previous one? -- [ ] Do decomposition terms **actually sum to the whole**? -- [ ] Are expectations, sums, and integrals applied correctly? -- [ ] Are indicator functions and conditioning events handled correctly? -- [ ] For matrix expressions: do dimensions match? -- [ ] Does the final result match what the cited paper actually proves? - ---- - -## Lens 3: Citation Fidelity - -For every claim attributed to a specific paper: - -- [ ] Does the slide accurately represent what the cited paper says? -- [ ] Is the result attributed to the **correct paper**? -- [ ] Is the theorem/proposition number correct (if cited)? -- [ ] Are "X (Year) show that..." statements actually things that paper shows? - -**Cross-reference with:** -- The project bibliography file -- Papers in `master_supporting_docs/supporting_papers/` (if available) -- The knowledge base in `.claude/rules/` (if it has a notation/citation registry) - ---- - -## Lens 4: Code-Theory Alignment - -When scripts exist for the lecture: - -- [ ] Does the code implement the exact formula shown on slides? -- [ ] Are the variables in the code the same ones the theory conditions on? -- [ ] Do model specifications match what's assumed on slides? -- [ ] Are standard errors computed using the method the slides describe? -- [ ] Do simulations match the paper being replicated? - - - - ---- - -## Lens 5: Backward Logic Check - -Read the lecture backwards — from conclusion to setup: - -- [ ] Starting from the final "takeaway" slide: is every claim supported by earlier content? -- [ ] Starting from each estimator: can you trace back to the identification result that justifies it? -- [ ] Starting from each identification result: can you trace back to the assumptions? -- [ ] Starting from each assumption: was it motivated and illustrated? -- [ ] Are there circular arguments? -- [ ] Would a student reading only slides N through M have the prerequisites for what's shown? - ---- - -## Cross-Lecture Consistency - -Check the target lecture against the knowledge base: - -- [ ] All notation matches the project's notation conventions -- [ ] Claims about previous lectures are accurate -- [ ] Forward pointers to future lectures are reasonable -- [ ] The same term means the same thing across lectures - ---- - -## Report Format - -Save report to `quality_reports/[FILENAME_WITHOUT_EXT]_substance_review.md`: - -```markdown -# Substance Review: [Filename] -**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD] -**Reviewer:** domain-reviewer agent - -## Summary -- **Overall assessment:** [SOUND / MINOR ISSUES / MAJOR ISSUES / CRITICAL ERRORS] -- **Total issues:** N -- **Blocking issues (prevent teaching):** M -- **Non-blocking issues (should fix when possible):** K - -## Lens 1: Assumption Stress Test -### Issues Found: N -#### Issue 1.1: [Brief title] -- **Slide:** [slide number or title] -- **Severity:** [CRITICAL / MAJOR / MINOR] -- **Claim on slide:** [exact text or equation] -- **Problem:** [what's missing, wrong, or insufficient] -- **Suggested fix:** [specific correction] - -## Lens 2: Derivation Verification -[Same format...] - -## Lens 3: Citation Fidelity -[Same format...] - -## Lens 4: Code-Theory Alignment -[Same format...] - -## Lens 5: Backward Logic Check -[Same format...] - -## Cross-Lecture Consistency -[Details...] - -## Critical Recommendations (Priority Order) -1. **[CRITICAL]** [Most important fix] -2. **[MAJOR]** [Second priority] - -## Positive Findings -[2-3 things the deck gets RIGHT — acknowledge rigor where it exists] -``` - ---- - -## Important Rules - -1. **NEVER edit source files.** Report only. -2. **Be precise.** Quote exact equations, slide titles, line numbers. -3. **Be fair.** Lecture slides simplify by design. Don't flag pedagogical simplifications as errors unless they're misleading. -4. **Distinguish levels:** CRITICAL = math is wrong. MAJOR = missing assumption or misleading. MINOR = could be clearer. -5. **Check your own work.** Before flagging an "error," verify your correction is correct. -6. **Respect the instructor.** Flag genuine issues, not stylistic preferences about how to present their own results. -7. **Read the knowledge base.** Check notation conventions before flagging "inconsistencies." diff --git a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/pedagogy-reviewer.md b/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/pedagogy-reviewer.md deleted file mode 100644 index e63dfa8..0000000 --- a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/pedagogy-reviewer.md +++ /dev/null @@ -1,159 +0,0 @@ ---- -name: pedagogy-reviewer -description: Holistic pedagogical review for academic slides. Checks narrative arc, prerequisite assumptions, worked examples, notation clarity, and deck-level pacing. Use after content is drafted. -tools: Read, Grep, Glob -model: inherit ---- - -You are an expert pedagogy reviewer for academic lecture slides. Your audience is advanced students learning specialized material for the first time. - -## Your Task - -Review the entire slide deck holistically. Produce a pedagogical report covering narrative arc, pacing, notation clarity, and student preparation. **Do NOT edit any files.** - -## 13 Pedagogical Patterns to Validate - -### 1. MOTIVATION BEFORE FORMALISM -- Every new concept MUST start with "Why?" before "What?" -- Pattern: Motivating slide → Definition → Worked example -- **Red flag:** Formal definition appears without context or motivation - -### 2. INCREMENTAL NOTATION -- Never introduce 5+ new symbols on a single slide -- Build notation progressively: simple → subscripted → full notation -- **Red flag:** Complex notation appears before simpler versions have been established - -### 3. WORKED EXAMPLE AFTER EVERY DEFINITION -- Every formal definition/assumption MUST have a concrete example within 2 slides -- **Red flag:** Two consecutive definition slides with no example between them - -### 4. PROGRESSIVE COMPLEXITY -- Order of presentation: simple → relative → distributional → conditional -- **Red flag:** Advanced concept introduced before simpler prerequisite - -### 5. FRAGMENT REVEALS FOR PROBLEM → SOLUTION -- Use `. . .` (Quarto) to create pedagogical moments -- Pattern: State problem → [fragment] → Show solution -- Target: 3-5 fragment reveals per lecture (not every slide — use sparingly) -- **Red flag:** Dense theorem slide reveals everything at once when incremental revelation would help - -### 6. STANDOUT SLIDES AT CONCEPTUAL PIVOTS -- Major transitions need a visual/thematic break (transition slide) -- **Red flag:** Abrupt jump from topic A to topic B with no transition - -### 7. TWO-SLIDE STRATEGY FOR DENSE THEOREMS -- Slide 1: Decomposition/statement with visual aids (`\underbrace{}`, color coding) -- Slide 2: Unpacking each term with intuition and plain-English interpretation -- Forward pointer on Slide 1: "(Each quantity defined on the next slide.)" -- **Red flag:** Single slide cramming a complex theorem plus all definitions - -### 8. SEMANTIC COLOR USAGE -- Use consistent colors for semantic meaning (e.g., green = good, red = bad, gray = context) -- **Red flag:** Binary contrasts shown in the same color - -### 9. BOX HIERARCHY -- Use different box types for different purposes (definitions, highlights, key results, quotes) -- **Red flag:** Wrong box type for content; quotebox without attribution - -### 10. BOX FATIGUE (PER-SLIDE) -- Maximum 1-2 colored boxes per slide -- More than 2 dilutes visual emphasis — demote transitional remarks to plain italic -- **Red flag:** 3 colored boxes on one slide - -### 11. SOCRATIC EMBEDDING -- Questions posed at bottom of slides to provoke thought -- Target: 2-3 embedded questions per lecture -- **Red flag:** Entire deck has zero questions — feels like a monologue, not a dialogue - -### 12. VISUAL-FIRST FOR COMPLEX CONCEPTS -- Show diagram / figure BEFORE introducing the formal notation when possible -- **Red flag:** Notation before the visualization has been shown - -### 13. TWO-COLUMN DEFINITION COMPARISONS -- When two related concepts are introduced, present them **side-by-side** rather than on consecutive slides -- The unifying takeaway below the columns ties the comparison together -- **Use when:** The comparison IS the pedagogical point -- **Red flag:** Two consecutive definition slides for closely related concepts that would be clearer side-by-side - -## Deck-Level Checks - -### NARRATIVE ARC -- Does the deck tell a coherent story from start to finish? -- Is there a clear progression (motivation → framework → methods → application)? -- Does the conclusion/takeaway slide tie back to the opening motivation? - -### PACING -- Count consecutive theory-heavy slides (max 3-4 before an example, application, or breather) -- Check for visual rhythm: Dense → Example → Dense → Application -- Transition slides appear at major conceptual pivots - -### VISUAL RHYTHM -- Section dividers appear every 5-8 slides -- Balance of text-heavy vs visual-heavy slides -- Not too many dense slides in a row - -### BOX FATIGUE (DECK-LEVEL) -- Total `.resultbox` count ≤ 3 per lecture -- No more than ~50% of slides have colored boxes -- Boxes reserved for genuinely important content - -### NOTATION CONSISTENCY -- Same symbol used consistently throughout the deck -- Cross-reference earlier lectures if they exist -- Check the knowledge base (`.claude/rules/`) for notation conventions - -### PRE-EMPTING STUDENT CONCERNS -- Would a student with standard prerequisites follow the presentation? -- Are common objections addressed? -- Are the limitations of each method acknowledged? -- Is it clear when assumptions are strong vs mild? - -## Report Format - -```markdown -# Pedagogical Review: [Filename] -**Date:** [date] -**Reviewer:** pedagogy-reviewer agent - -## Summary -- **Patterns followed:** X/13 -- **Patterns violated:** Y/13 -- **Patterns partially applied:** Z/13 -- **Deck-level assessment:** [Brief overall verdict] - -## Pattern-by-Pattern Assessment - -### Pattern 1: Motivation Before Formalism -- **Status:** [Followed / Violated / Partially Applied] -- **Evidence:** [Specific slide titles or line numbers] -- **Recommendation:** [How to improve, if violated] -- **Severity:** [High / Medium / Low] - -[Repeat for all 13 patterns...] - -## Deck-Level Analysis - -### Narrative Arc -[Free-form assessment] - -### Pacing -[Assessment of theory/example balance] - -### Visual Rhythm -[Section divider frequency, text vs visual balance] - -### Notation Consistency -[Cross-lecture notation check] - -### Student Concerns -[Potential objections or confusions] - -## Critical Recommendations (Top 3-5) -1. [Most important improvement] -2. [Second most important] -3. [Third most important] -``` - -## Save Location - -Save the report to: `quality_reports/[FILENAME_WITHOUT_EXT]_pedagogy_report.md` diff --git a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/proofreader.md b/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/proofreader.md deleted file mode 100644 index 5cb5bd9..0000000 --- a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/proofreader.md +++ /dev/null @@ -1,65 +0,0 @@ ---- -name: proofreader -description: Expert proofreading agent for academic lecture slides. Reviews for grammar, typos, overflow, and consistency. Use proactively after creating or modifying lecture content. -tools: Read, Grep, Glob -model: inherit ---- - -You are an expert proofreading agent for academic papers. - -## Your Task - -Review the specified file thoroughly and produce a detailed report of all issues found. **Do NOT edit any files.** Only produce the report. - -## Check for These Categories - -### 1. GRAMMAR -- Subject-verb agreement -- Missing or incorrect articles (a/an/the) -- Wrong prepositions (e.g., "eligible to" → "eligible for") -- Tense consistency within and across slides -- Dangling modifiers - -### 2. TYPOS -- Misspellings -- Search-and-replace artifacts (e.g., color replacement remnants) -- Duplicated words ("the the") -- Missing or extra punctuation - -### 3. OVERFLOW -- **LaTeX (.tex):** Content likely to cause overfull hbox warnings. Look for long equations without `\resizebox`, overly long bullet points, or too many items per slide. -- **Quarto (.qmd):** Content likely to exceed slide boundaries. Look for: too many bullet points, inline font-size overrides below 0.85em, missing negative margins on dense slides. - -### 4. CONSISTENCY -- Citation format: `\citet` vs `\citep` (LaTeX), `@key` vs `[@key]` (Quarto) -- Notation: Same symbol used for different things, or different symbols for the same thing -- Terminology: Consistent use of terms across slides -- Box usage: `keybox` vs `highlightbox` vs `methodbox` used appropriately - -### 5. ACADEMIC QUALITY -- Informal abbreviations (don't, can't, it's) -- Missing words that make sentences incomplete -- Awkward phrasing that could confuse students -- Claims without citations -- Citations pointing to the wrong paper -- Verify that citation keys match the intended paper in the bibliography file - -## Report Format - -For each issue found, provide: - -```markdown -### Issue N: [Brief description] -- **File:** [filename] -- **Location:** [slide title or line number] -- **Current:** "[exact text that's wrong]" -- **Proposed:** "[exact text with fix]" -- **Category:** [Grammar / Typo / Overflow / Consistency / Academic Quality] -- **Severity:** [High / Medium / Low] -``` - -## Save the Report - -Save to `quality_reports/[FILENAME_WITHOUT_EXT]_report.md` - -For `.qmd` files, append `_qmd` to the name: `quality_reports/[FILENAME]_qmd_report.md` diff --git a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/quarto-critic.md b/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/quarto-critic.md deleted file mode 100644 index 5804acb..0000000 --- a/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/agents/quarto-critic.md +++ /dev/null @@ -1,170 +0,0 @@ ---- -name: quarto-critic -description: Adversarial QA agent that compares Quarto HTML against Beamer PDF benchmark. Produces harsh, actionable criticism. Does NOT edit files — read-only analysis only. -tools: Read, Grep, Glob -model: inherit ---- - -You are a **harsh, uncompromising quality auditor** for academic presentation slides. - -Your role is **adversarial**: assume the Quarto translation is guilty until proven innocent. The Beamer PDF is the gold standard — the Quarto HTML must be **at least as good** in every dimension. - -## Your Task - -Compare the Quarto HTML slides against the Beamer PDF benchmark. Produce a detailed comparison report identifying ALL deficiencies. **Do NOT edit any files — you are read-only.** - ---- - -## Hard Gates (Non-Negotiable) - -If ANY of these fail, the verdict is **REJECTED**: - -| Gate | Condition | How to Check | -|------|-----------|--------------| -| **Overflow** | ANY content cut off or requiring scroll | Read QMD, check for dense slides; grep for `.smaller` class usage | -| **Plot Quality** | Chart uglier/less readable than Beamer | Compare static plots vs interactive versions | -| **Content Parity** | Missing slides, equations, or key text | Count frames in Beamer vs slides in QMD | -| **Visual Regression** | Quarto looks worse than Beamer in any dimension | Check boxes, spacing, typography | -| **Slide Centering** | Content must be centered; no jumping between slides | Check for consistent vertical positioning | -| **Notation Fidelity** | ALL mathematical notation MUST be verbatim from Beamer | Compare every `$...$` and `$$...$$` — NO placeholders, NO abbreviations | -| **Equation Formatting** | Line breaks and equation alignment MUST match Beamer quality | Compare multi-line equations, alignment environments | - ---- - -## Comparison Dimensions - -### 1. Content Fidelity (HARD GATE) - -**Check every single element:** -- **Slide count:** Beamer frames ≈ Quarto slides (±2 for section headers) -- **Equations:** Every equation in Beamer must appear verbatim in Quarto -- **Bullet points:** Every item preserved, same hierarchy -- **Citations:** Every citation present with correct key -- **No summarization:** Quarto must NOT condense or rephrase Beamer content - -### 1b. Notation Fidelity (HARD GATE — CRITICAL) - -**ZERO TOLERANCE for notation differences.** Mathematical notation must be VERBATIM from Beamer. - -**Check for these violations:** -- `\cdots` or `...` placeholders where Beamer has full expressions -- Missing subscripts (`X` instead of `X_i`) -- Missing function arguments -- Simplified fractions (inline `/` instead of `\frac{}{}`) -- Missing `\mathbb{}`, `\boldsymbol{}`, or other formatting commands - -### 1c. Equation Formatting & Line Breaks (HARD GATE — CRITICAL) - -**Quarto equations must be AT LEAST as readable as Beamer.** - -**Check for:** -- Displayed equations in Beamer that became inline in Quarto -- Multi-line equations reduced to single line -- Missing alignment points -- Missing spacing commands - -### 2. Overflow Check (HARD GATE) - -**Check for overflow indicators in the QMD:** -- `{style="font-size: 0.8em"}` or smaller -- `.smaller` or `.smallest` class on non-appendix slides -- Multiple boxes on one slide (crowding) -- Content after plotly charts (must be last element) - -### 3. Visual Quality Comparison - -- Plots: same information, similar readability? -- TikZ: SVGs referenced (not PDFs)? -- Tables: same structure, alignment? -- Boxes: every Beamer box type has CSS equivalent? - -### 4. Typography & Spacing - -- Font-size reductions below 0.85em? -- Inconsistent heading styles? -- Adequate whitespace? - -### 5. Semantic Fidelity - -- Correct usage of semantic color classes -- Correct emphasis matchin

input.content:
# Session Log: Interface Design Research in IS - Slide Creation

**Date:** 2026-03-22
**Task:** Create full Beamer slide deck from research document `Interface design research in IS - MY.docx`
**Status:** COMPLETED

---

## Task Summary

Created a comprehensive 21-page Beamer presentation covering the research narrative on visual interface design and its effects on investor behavior and decision-making.

## Files Created

| File | Location | Purpose |
|------|----------|---------|
| `interface_design_slides.tex` | `/Users/my/output/Interface_Design_IS/` | Main Beamer source |
| `interface_design_slides.pdf` | `/Users/my/output/Interface_Design_IS/` | Compiled output (21 pages, 209KB) |

## Slide Structure

1. **Title slide** - Interface Design Research in Information Systems
2. **Motivation: Morningstar UX redesign case** - Real-world example of color-coded risk metrics
3. **Research Question & Dual Positioning** - IS and Finance research streams
4. **Research Gap** - What existing literature does not study
5. **Contributions** - Three-pillar framework: cognition, attention, market consequences
6. **Transition** - Literature Review: Website Design and Cognition
7. **Literature 1** - Visual Effects on Attention (Deng & Poole 2010; Hong et al. 2004; Speier & Morris 2003)
8. **Literature 2** - Visual Cues and Integration (Kim et al. 2000; Cyr 2008; Jiang et al. 2016)
9. **Literature 3** - Complexity and Cognitive Fit (Hong et al. 2004; Nadkarni & Gupta 2007; Hu et al. 2017)
10. **Transition** - Information, Marketing & Actions Support
11. **Literature 4** - Benbasat & Dexter 1985; Dennis et al. 2020; Li et al. 2023; Pieters et al. 2007
12. **Literature 5** - Visual Analytics and Social Media (Shin et al. 2020; Dou et al. 2010; Sim et al. 2024)
13. **Transition** - Neurophysiological Approaches
14. **Literature 6** - Lakhiwal et al. 2023; Vance et al. 2025; Meservy et al. 2019
15. **Transition** - Finance Positioning: Visual Risk Metrics
16. **Finance Literature** - Visual finance papers (Grant et al. 2024; Cardinaels et al. 2024; Fisher 2021/2023)
17. **Implications and Future Directions** - Research and practical implications
18. **References** - Full citation list with journal names
19. **Closing slide** - Thank You / Questions

## Quality Verification

- **LaTeX compilation:** 3-pass pdflatex completed successfully
- **No overfull hbox warnings:** Checked log file, clean
- **Citations:** Full format [Author, Year, Journal] as requested
- **Colored blocks:** Maximum 2 per slide (mostly 1 per content slide)
- **No `\pause` commands:** As per constraints

## Notes

- Used Madrid theme with default color scheme for academic presentation
- All literature review citations extracted from the source Word document
- Preserved dual positioning (IS vs Finance) narrative structure
- 21 total pages including title, transitions, references, and closing

## Next Steps (Optional)

- User may want to add figures/screenshots of Morningstar interface
- Consider adding TikZ diagrams for the IS-Cognitive lens framework
- Run `/slide-excellence` or `/visual-audit` for detailed layout review if needed

content.content: File created successfully at: /Users/my/quality_reports/session_interface_design_2026-03-22.md

1→# Skill Creation Template
     2→
     3→**Use this template to create domain-specific skills for your academic workflow.**
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## When to Create a Custom Skill
     8→
     9→Create a skill when you find yourself:
    10→- Repeatedly explaining the same 3+ step workflow to Claude
    11→- Needing domain-specific quality checks (citation style, notation consistency, lab protocols)
    12→- Enforcing field-specific output formats (thesis structure, journal templates, lab notebooks)
    13→- Coordinating multi-tool workflows (Figma → R → LaTeX, data → analysis → manuscript)
    14→
    15→**Don't create a skill for:**
    16→- One-time tasks
    17→- Workflows that change frequently
    18→- Simple 1-2 step operations
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## Template Structure
    23→
    24→Copy the structure below to `.claude/skills/[your-skill-name]/SKILL.md`:
    25→
    26→```markdown
    27→---
    28→name: your-skill-name
    29→description: [What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]. Use when user asks for "[trigger phrase 1]", "[trigger phrase 2]", or "[context]".
    30→argument-hint: "[brief hint for user]"
    31→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Edit", "Grep", "Glob", "Bash", "Task"]
    32→disable-model-invocation: true
    33→---
    34→
    35→# [Skill Name]
    36→
    37→[One sentence: what this skill accomplishes and why it exists]
    38→
    39→## Instructions
    40→
    41→Step 1: [First major action with clear explanation]
    42→   - Detail: [Important consideration]
    43→   - Example: [Concrete example]
    44→
    45→Step 2: [Second major action]
    46→   - Detail: [Important consideration]
    47→
    48→Step 3: [Final action and verification]
    49→   - Verify: [What to check]
    50→   - Output: [What user receives]
    51→
    52→## Examples
    53→
    54→### Example 1: [Common Scenario Name]
    55→**Context:** [When this occurs]
    56→**User says:** "[Typical user request]"
    57→**Actions:**
    58→1. [What skill does first]
    59→2. [What skill does second]
    60→3. [Final output]
    61→**Result:** [What user receives]
    62→
    63→### Example 2: [Another Common Scenario]
    64→**Context:** [Different situation]
    65→**User says:** "[Alternative phrasing]"
    66→**Actions:**
    67→1. [Different path through the skill]
    68→**Result:** [Expected outcome]
    69→
    70→## Troubleshooting
    71→
    72→**Error:** [Common error message or symptom]
    73→**Cause:** [Why this happens]
    74→**Solution:** [How to fix it]
    75→
    76→**Error:** [Another common issue]
    77→**Cause:** [Root cause]
    78→**Solution:** [Fix steps]
    79→```
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## Writing Effective Descriptions
    84→
    85→The `description` field determines when Claude loads your skill. Use this structure:
    86→
    87→```
    88→[What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]
    89→```
    90→
    91→### Good Examples (Field-Specific)
    92→
    93→**Citation Style Enforcement (APA Psychology):**
    94→```yaml
    95→description: Enforces APA 7th edition citation format in manuscripts. Use when user asks to "check citations", "fix references", "apply APA style", or when reviewing .tex or .qmd files with bibliography. Checks author-year format, DOI formatting, and reference list completeness.
    96→```
    97→
    98→**Lab Notebook Entry Generator (Wet Lab Biology):**
    99→```yaml
   100→description: Generates structured lab notebook entries from experimental notes. Use when user provides "experiment notes", "protocol results", or asks to "format lab entry". Ensures date, hypothesis, materials, procedure, observations, and conclusions are documented.
   101→```
   102→
   103→**Thesis Chapter Structure Checker (Graduate Students):**
   104→```yaml
   105→description: Validates thesis chapter structure against institutional requirements. Use when user asks to "check chapter format", "validate thesis structure", or when editing thesis .tex or .docx files. Verifies required sections, heading levels, and citation density.
   106→```
   107→
   108→**Econometric Specification Review (Economics):**
   109→```yaml
   110→description: Reviews econometric specifications for common errors. Use when user shares regression code in R or Stata, or asks to "check model spec", "review estimation". Validates: standard error clustering, fixed effects structure, missing covariates, and replication commands.
   111→```
   112→
   113→### Bad Examples (Too Generic)
   114→
   115→❌ `description: Helps with citations`
   116→→ Too vague, doesn't specify style or when to trigger
   117→
   118→❌ `description: Checks lab notebooks`
   119→→ Missing trigger phrases, no format specification
   120→
   121→❌ `description: Reviews thesis chapters`
   122→→ Doesn't specify what aspects or when to activate
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## Academic Domain Examples
   127→
   128→### Example 1: Citation Cross-Reference Checker
   129→
   130→**File:** `.claude/skills/validate-citations/SKILL.md`
   131→
   132→```markdown
   133→---
   134→name: validate-citations
   135→description: Cross-references in-text citations against bibliography entries. Use when user asks to "check citations", "validate references", or when working on .tex, .qmd, or .md files with bibliographies. Identifies missing entries, unused references, and formatting inconsistencies.
   136→argument-hint: "[file or 'all']"
   137→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Write"]
   138→---
   139→
   140→# Validate Citations
   141→
   142→Ensures all in-text citations have corresponding bibliography entries and identifies unused references.
   143→
   144→## Instructions
   145→
   146→Step 1: **Extract in-text citations**
   147→   - Use Grep to find citation commands: `\cite{...}`, `\citep{...}`, `[@...]`
   148→   - Build list of cited keys
   149→
   150→Step 2: **Parse bibliography file**
   151→   - Read Bibliography_base.bib (or specified .bib file)
   152→   - Extract all `@article{key,`, `@book{key,` entries
   153→
   154→Step 3: **Cross-reference**
   155→   - Missing in bib: citations without entries
   156→   - Unused in bib: entries never cited
   157→
   158→Step 4: **Report findings**
   159→   - Save to `quality_reports/citation_validation.md`
   160→   - List missing entries (CRITICAL)
   161→   - List unused entries (for cleanup)
   162→   - Check DOI formatting if present
   163→
   164→## Examples
   165→
   166→### Example 1: Manuscript with Missing Citation
   167→**User says:** "Check if all my citations are in the bib file"
   168→**Actions:**
   169→1. Grep for `\citep{` in manuscript.tex
   170→2. Extract keys: `Smith2020`, `Jones2019`, `Lee2021`
   171→3. Parse Bibliography_base.bib
   172→4. Find `Lee2021` missing
   173→**Result:** Report: "Missing entry: Lee2021. Add to bibliography before submission."
   174→
   175→## Troubleshooting
   176→
   177→**Error:** Citation format not recognized
   178→**Cause:** Non-standard citation command
   179→**Solution:** Add pattern to Step 1 grep (e.g., `\citeauthor`, `\citeyear`)
   180→```
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→### Example 2: Regression Output Formatter
   185→
   186→**File:** `.claude/skills/format-regression-tables/SKILL.md`
   187→
   188→```markdown
   189→---
   190→name: format-regression-tables
   191→description: Converts R regression outputs to publication-ready LaTeX tables. Use when user runs regressions and says "make a table", "format results", or "export to LaTeX". Handles lm, glm, felm, and fixest objects. Applies field-specific conventions (standard errors in parentheses, stars for significance).
   192→argument-hint: "[model object name]"
   193→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash"]
   194→---
   195→
   196→# Format Regression Tables
   197→
   198→Converts R regression objects into publication-ready LaTeX tables with proper formatting.
   199→
   200→## Instructions
   201→
   202→Step 1: **Identify model objects**
   203→   - User provides R object names (e.g., `model1`, `model2`)
   204→   - Read corresponding .rds files from output/
   205→
   206→Step 2: **Extract coefficients and statistics**
   207→   - Coefficient estimates
   208→   - Standard errors (in parentheses)
   209→   - Significance stars: * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01
   210→   - R-squared, N observations
   211→
   212→Step 3: **Generate LaTeX table**
   213→   - Use booktabs style (no vertical lines)
   214→   - Align on decimal points
   215→   - Add notes for standard errors and significance
   216→
   217→Step 4: **Save and verify**
   218→   - Save to `output/tables/regression_results.tex`
   219→   - Verify compiles in standalone .tex document
   220→
   221→## Examples
   222→
   223→### Example 1: Panel Regression with Fixed Effects
   224→**User says:** "Format my regression results for the paper"
   225→**Context:** User has run `felm(y ~ x | firm + year | 0 | firm, data=df)`
   226→**Actions:**
   227→1. Read saved model object
   228→2. Extract: coefficients, clustered SEs, R², N
   229→3. Generate LaTeX:
   230→   ```latex
   231→   \begin{table}[htbp]
   232→   \caption{Effect of X on Y}
   233→   \begin{tabular}{lc}
   234→   \toprule
   235→   & (1) \\
   236→   \midrule
   237→   X & 0.523*** \\
   238→     & (0.089) \\
   239→   \midrule
   240→   Firm FE & Yes \\
   241→   Year FE & Yes \\
   242→   \midrule
   243→   Observations & 1,234 \\
   244→   R-squared & 0.678 \\
   245→   \bottomrule
   246→   \multicolumn{2}{l}{\footnotesize Clustered SEs in parentheses.} \\
   247→   \multicolumn{2}{l}{\footnotesize * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01}
   248→   \end{tabular}
   249→   \end{table}
   250→   ```
   251→**Result:** Ready to `\input` in manuscript.tex
   252→
   253→## Troubleshooting
   254→
   255→**Error:** Model object not found
   256→**Cause:** .rds file not in expected location
   257→**Solution:** Check output/ directory, verify saveRDS() was called
   258→
   259→**Error:** Standard errors missing
   260→**Cause:** Model didn't specify clustering/robust SEs
   261→**Solution:** Re-run with vcov specification, document assumption
   262→```
   263→
   264→---
   265→
   266→### Example 3: Experimental Protocol Validator
   267→
   268→**File:** `.claude/skills/validate-protocol/SKILL.md`
   269→
   270→```markdown
   271→---
   272→name: validate-protocol
   273→description: Validates experimental protocols against lab safety and reproducibility standards. Use when user provides lab protocol documents, asks to "check protocol", "validate procedure", or when reviewing .md or .docx experimental designs. Ensures required sections, safety notes, and replication details are present.
   274→argument-hint: "[protocol file]"
   275→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write"]
   276→---
   277→
   278→# Validate Experimental Protocol
   279→
   280→Checks experimental protocols for completeness, safety documentation, and reproducibility.
   281→
   282→## Instructions
   283→
   284→Step 1: **Parse protocol document**
   285→   - Extract sections: Objective, Materials, Procedure, Safety, Expected Results
   286→   - Check for required metadata: Date, Version, Author
   287→
   288→Step 2: **Validate required elements**
   289→   - Materials list complete (catalog numbers, suppliers)
   290→   - Safety warnings for hazardous materials
   291→   - Quantitative measurements (volumes, temperatures, times)
   292→   - Controls specified
   293→
   294→Step 3: **Reproducibility check**
   295→   - Equipment specifications listed
   296→   - Randomization described (if applicable)
   297→   - Sample sizes justified
   298→   - Data recording method specified
   299→
   300→Step 4: **Generate report**
   301→   - PASS/FAIL for each section
   302→   - Missing elements highlighted
   303→   - Suggestions for improvement
   304→
   305→## Examples
   306→
   307→### Example 1: Cell Culture Protocol
   308→**User says:** "Check if my protocol is complete before submitting to IRB"
   309→**Context:** Protocol for isolating primary neurons
   310→**Actions:**
   311→1. Read protocol.md
   312→2. Check Materials: ✓ catalog numbers present
   313→3. Check Safety: ✗ missing biosafety level designation
   314→4. Check Procedure: ✓ quantitative (37°C, 5% CO₂, 10 mL media)
   315→**Result:** Report: "Missing: BSL-2 designation. Add before IRB submission."
   316→
   317→## Troubleshooting
   318→
   319→**Error:** Cannot parse sections
   320→**Cause:** Non-standard heading format
   321→**Solution:** Add heading patterns to Step 1 (e.g., "### Safety Notes")
   322→```
   323→
   324→---
   325→
   326→## Testing Your Skill
   327→
   328→### Step 1: Manual Test
   329→1. Create skill directory: `mkdir -p .claude/skills/your-skill-name`
   330→2. Copy SKILL.md template and customize
   331→3. Restart Claude Code or run `/reload` if available
   332→4. Trigger skill: Use one of your trigger phrases
   333→5. Verify: Skill loads, instructions are clear, output is correct
   334→
   335→### Step 2: Iteration
   336→- **If skill doesn't trigger:** Revise description with more specific trigger phrases
   337→- **If instructions unclear:** Add more examples and detail to Steps
   338→- **If output wrong:** Add validation steps, troubleshooting section
   339→
   340→### Step 3: Success Criteria
   341→- ✅ Skill triggers on 90%+ of relevant queries
   342→- ✅ Complete workflow in expected number of steps
   343→- ✅ Zero API errors during normal operation
   344→- ✅ Same task yields consistent outputs across sessions
   345→
   346→---
   347→
   348→## Field-Specific Customization Checklist
   349→
   350→When adapting this template to your domain:
   351→
   352→- [ ] Replace example trigger phrases with your field's terminology
   353→- [ ] Add domain-specific file types (`.R`, `.py`, `.ipynb`, `.tex`, `.stan`)
   354→- [ ] Include field conventions (notation, formatting, citation styles)
   355→- [ ] Reference standard tools (`ggplot2`, `pandas`, `TikZ`, `Stata`)
   356→- [ ] Add common error messages from your toolchain
   357→- [ ] Include institutional requirements (thesis formats, journal templates)
   358→
   359→---
   360→
   361→## Allowed Tools Reference
   362→
   363→| Tool | Use For |
   364→|------|---------|
   365→| `Read` | Reading file contents (scripts, manuscripts, data) |
   366→| `Write` | Creating new files (reports, tables, outputs) |
   367→| `Edit` | Modifying existing files in place |
   368→| `Grep` | Searching file contents (citations, function names) |
   369→| `Glob` | Finding files by pattern (*.R, *.tex, *.csv) |
   370→| `Bash` | Running commands (R scripts, LaTeX compilation, git) |
   371→| `Task` | Launching subagents (for complex multi-step workflows) |
   372→
   373→**Security note:** Only grant `Bash` access if your skill needs to execute code or compile documents. For read-only validation skills, omit it.

1→# My Claude Code Setup 2→ 3→> **Work in progress.** This is not meant to be a polished guide for everyone. It's mostly a summary of how I've been using Claude Code for academic work — creating lecture slides, writing R scripts, managing Beamer-to-Quarto workflows, and so on. I keep learning new things, and as I do, I keep updating these files. This is just a way for me to share what I've figured out with friends and colleagues. 4→ 5→**Live site:** [psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/) 6→**Last Updated:** 2026-02-15 7→ 8→A ready-to-fork starter kit for academics using [Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) with **LaTeX/Beamer + R + Quarto**. You describe what you want; Claude plans the approach, runs specialized agents, fixes issues, verifies quality, and presents results — like a contractor who handles the entire job. Extracted from a production PhD course (6 lectures, 800+ slides). 9→ 10→--- 11→ 12→## Quick Start (5 minutes) 13→ 14→### 1. Fork & Clone 15→ 16→```bash 17→# Fork this repo on GitHub (click "Fork" on the repo page), then: 18→git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/claude-code-my-workflow.git my-project 19→cd my-project 20→``` 21→ 22→Replace `YOUR_USERNAME` with your GitHub username. 23→ 24→### 2. Start Claude Code and Paste This Prompt 25→ 26→```bash 27→claude 28→``` 29→ 30→**Using VS Code?** Open the Claude Code panel instead. Everything works the same — see the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for details. 31→ 32→Then paste the following, filling in your project details: 33→ 34→> I am starting to work on **[PROJECT NAME]** in this repo. **[Describe your project in 2–3 sentences — what you're building, who it's for, what tools you use.]** 35→> 36→> I want our collaboration to be structured, precise, and rigorous. When creating visuals, everything must be polished and publication-ready. 37→> 38→> I've set up the Claude Code academic workflow (forked from `pedrohcgs/claude-code-my-workflow`). The configuration files are already in this repo. Please read them, understand the workflow, and then **update all configuration files to fit my project** — fill in placeholders in `CLAUDE.md`, adjust rules if needed, and propose any customizations specific to my use case. 39→> 40→> After that, use the plan-first workflow for all non-trivial tasks. Once I approve a plan, switch to contractor mode — coordinate everything autonomously and only come back to me when there's ambiguity or a decision to make. 41→> 42→> Enter plan mode and start by adapting the workflow configuration for this project. 43→ 44→**What this does:** Claude reads all the configuration files, fills in your project name, institution, and preferences, then enters contractor mode — planning, implementing, reviewing, and verifying autonomously. You approve the plan and Claude handles the rest. 45→ 46→**Prefer to configure manually?** See the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for step-by-step manual setup instructions. 47→ 48→--- 49→ 50→## How It Works 51→ 52→### Contractor Mode 53→ 54→You describe a task. For complex or ambiguous requests, Claude first creates a requirements specification with MUST/SHOULD/MAY priorities and clarity status (CLEAR/ASSUMED/BLOCKED). You approve the spec, then Claude plans the approach, implements it, runs specialized review agents, fixes issues, re-verifies, and scores against quality gates — all autonomously. You see a summary when the work meets quality standards. Say "just do it" and it auto-commits too. 55→ 56→### Specialized Agents 57→ 58→Instead of one general-purpose reviewer, 10 focused agents each check one dimension: 59→ 60→- **proofreader** — grammar/typos 61→- **slide-auditor** — visual layout 62→- **pedagogy-reviewer** — teaching quality 63→- **r-reviewer** — R code quality 64→- **domain-reviewer** — field-specific correctness (template — customize for your field) 65→ 66→Each is better at its narrow task than a generalist would be. The `/slide-excellence` skill runs them all in parallel. 67→ 68→### Adversarial QA 69→ 70→Two agents work in opposition: the **critic** reads both Beamer and Quarto and produces harsh findings. The **fixer** implements exactly what the critic found. They loop until the critic says "APPROVED" (or 5 rounds max). This catches errors that single-pass review misses. 71→ 72→### Quality Gates 73→ 74→Every file gets a score (0–100). Scores below threshold block the action: 75→- **80** — commit threshold 76→- **90** — PR threshold 77→- **95** — excellence (aspirational) 78→ 79→### Context Survival 80→ 81→Plans, specifications, and session logs survive auto-compression and session boundaries. The PreCompact hook saves a context snapshot before Claude's auto-compression triggers, ensuring critical decisions are never lost. MEMORY.md accumulates learning across sessions, so patterns discovered in one session inform future work. 82→ 83→--- 84→ 85→## The Guide 86→ 87→For a comprehensive walkthrough, read the **[full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html)** (or see the [source](guide/workflow-guide.qmd)). 88→ 89→It covers: 90→1. **Why This Workflow Exists** — the problem and the vision 91→2. **Getting Started** — fork, paste one prompt, and Claude sets up the rest 92→3. **The System in Action** — specialized agents, adversarial QA, quality scoring 93→4. **The Building Blocks** — CLAUDE.md, rules, skills, agents, hooks, memory 94→5. **Workflow Patterns** — lecture creation, translation, replication, multi-agent review, research exploration 95→6. **Customizing for Your Domain** — creating your own reviewers and knowledge bases 96→ 97→--- 98→ 99→## What's Included 100→ 101→
102→10 agents, 21 skills, 17 rules, 7 hooks (click to expand) 103→ 104→### Agents (`.claude/agents/`) 105→ 106→| Agent | What It Does | 107→|-------|-------------| 108→| `proofreader` | Grammar, typos, overflow, consistency review | 109→| `slide-auditor` | Visual layout audit (overflow, font consistency, spacing) | 110→| `pedagogy-reviewer` | 13-pattern pedagogical review (narrative arc, notation density, pacing) | 111→| `r-reviewer` | R code quality, reproducibility, and domain correctness | 112→| `tikz-reviewer` | Merciless TikZ diagram visual critique | 113→| `beamer-translator` | Beamer-to-Quarto translation specialist | 114→| `quarto-critic` | Adversarial QA comparing Quarto against Beamer benchmark | 115→| `quarto-fixer` | Implements fixes from the critic agent | 116→| `verifier` | End-to-end task completion verification | 117→| `domain-reviewer` | **Template** for your field-specific substance reviewer | 118→ 119→### Skills (`.claude/skills/`) 120→ 121→| Skill | What It Does | 122→|-------|-------------| 123→| `/compile-latex` | 3-pass XeLaTeX compilation with bibtex | 124→| `/deploy` | Render Quarto + sync to GitHub Pages | 125→| `/extract-tikz` | TikZ diagrams to PDF to SVG pipeline | 126→| `/proofread` | Launch proofreader on a file | 127→| `/visual-audit` | Launch slide-auditor on a file | 128→| `/pedagogy-review` | Launch pedagogy-reviewer on a file | 129→| `/review-r` | Launch R code reviewer | 130→| `/qa-quarto` | Adversarial critic-fixer loop (max 5 rounds) | 131→| `/slide-excellence` | Combined multi-agent review | 132→| `/translate-to-quarto` | Full 11-phase Beamer-to-Quarto translation | 133→| `/validate-bib` | Cross-reference citations against bibliography | 134→| `/devils-advocate` | Challenge design decisions before committing | 135→| `/create-lecture` | Full lecture creation workflow | 136→| `/commit` | Stage, commit, create PR, and merge to main | 137→| `/lit-review` | Literature search, synthesis, and gap identification | 138→| `/research-ideation` | Generate research questions and empirical strategies | 139→| `/interview-me` | Interactive interview to formalize a research idea | 140→| `/review-paper` | Manuscript review: structure, econometrics, referee objections | 141→| `/data-analysis` | End-to-end R analysis with publication-ready output | 142→| `/learn` | Extract non-obvious discoveries into persistent skills | 143→| `/context-status` | Show session health and context usage | 144→ 145→### Research Workflow 146→ 147→| Feature | What It Does | 148→|---------|-------------| 149→| Exploration folder | Structured `explorations/` sandbox with graduate/archive lifecycle | 150→| Fast-track workflow | 60/100 quality threshold for rapid prototyping | 151→| Simplified orchestrator | implement → verify → score → done (no multi-round reviews) | 152→| Enhanced session logging | Structured tables for changes, decisions, verification | 153→| Merge-only reporting | Quality reports at merge time only | 154→| Math line-length exception | Long lines acceptable for documented formulas | 155→| Workflow quick reference | One-page cheat sheet at `.claude/WORKFLOW_QUICK_REF.md` | 156→ 157→### Rules (`.claude/rules/`) 158→ 159→Rules use path-scoped loading: **always-on** rules load every session (~100 lines total); **path-scoped** rules load only when Claude works on matching files. Claude follows ~150 instructions reliably, so less is more. 160→ 161→**Always-on** (no `paths:` frontmatter — load every session): 162→ 163→| Rule | What It Enforces | 164→|------|-----------------| 165→| `plan-first-workflow` | Plan mode for non-trivial tasks + context preservation | 166→| `orchestrator-protocol` | Contractor mode: implement → verify → review → fix → score | 167→| `session-logging` | Three logging triggers: post-plan, incremental, end-of-session | 168→ 169→**Path-scoped** (load only when working on matching files): 170→ 171→| Rule | Triggers On | What It Enforces | 172→|------|------------|-----------------| 173→| `verification-protocol` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `docs/` | Task completion checklist | 174→| `single-source-of-truth` | `Figures/`, `.tex`, `.qmd` | No content duplication; Beamer is authoritative | 175→| `quality-gates` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `*.R` | 80/90/95 scoring + tolerance thresholds | 176→| `r-code-conventions` | `*.R` | R coding standards + math line-length exception | 177→| `tikz-visual-quality` | `.tex` | TikZ diagram visual standards | 178→| `beamer-quarto-sync` | `.tex`, `.qmd` | Auto-sync Beamer edits to Quarto | 179→| `pdf-processing` | `master_supporting_docs/` | Safe large PDF handling | 180→| `proofreading-protocol` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `quality_reports/` | Propose-first, then apply with approval | 181→| `no-pause-beamer` | `.tex` | No overlay commands in Beamer | 182→| `replication-protocol` | `*.R` | Replicate original results before extending | 183→| `knowledge-base-template` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `*.R` | Notation/application registry template | 184→| `orchestrator-research` | `*.R`, `explorations/` | Simple orchestrator for research (no multi-round reviews) | 185→| `exploration-folder-protocol` | `explorations/` | Structured sandbox for experimental work | 186→| `exploration-fast-track` | `explorations/` | Lightweight exploration workflow (60/100 threshold) | 187→ 188→### Templates (`templates/`) 189→ 190→| Template | What It Does | 191→|----------|-------------| 192→| `session-log.md` | Structured session logging format | 193→| `quality-report.md` | Merge-time quality report format | 194→| `exploration-readme.md` | Exploration project README template | 195→| `archive-readme.md` | Archive documentation template | 196→| `requirements-spec.md` | MUST/SHOULD/MAY requirements framework with clarity status | 197→| `constitutional-governance.md` | Template for defining non-negotiable principles vs. preferences | 198→| `skill-template.md` | Academic skill creation template with domain-specific examples | 199→ 200→
201→ 202→--- 203→ 204→## Prerequisites 205→ 206→| Tool | Required For | Install | 207→|------|-------------|---------| 208→| [Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) | Everything | `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` | 209→| XeLaTeX | LaTeX compilation | [TeX Live](https://tug.org/texlive/) or [MacTeX](https://tug.org/mactex/) | 210→| [Quarto](https://quarto.org) | Web slides | [quarto.org/docs/get-started](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) | 211→| R | Figures & analysis | [r-project.org](https://www.r-project.org/) | 212→| pdf2svg | TikZ to SVG | `brew install pdf2svg` (macOS) | 213→| [gh CLI](https://cli.github.com/) | PR workflow | `brew install gh` (macOS) | 214→ 215→Not all tools are needed — install only what your project uses. Claude Code is the only hard requirement. 216→ 217→--- 218→ 219→## Adapting for Your Field 220→ 221→1. **Fill in the knowledge base** (`.claude/rules/knowledge-base-template.md`) with your notation, applications, and design principles 222→2. **Customize the domain reviewer** (`.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md`) with review lenses specific to your field 223→3. **Update the color palette** in your Quarto theme SCSS file — change the color variables at the top 224→4. **Add field-specific R pitfalls** to `.claude/rules/r-code-conventions.md` 225→5. **Fill in the lecture mapping** in `.claude/rules/beamer-quarto-sync.md` 226→6. **Customize the workflow quick reference** (`.claude/WORKFLOW_QUICK_REF.md`) with your non-negotiables and preferences 227→7. **Set up the exploration folder** (`explorations/`) for experimental work 228→ 229→--- 230→ 231→## Additional Resources 232→ 233→- [Claude Code Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) 234→- [Writing a Good CLAUDE.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory) — official guidance on project memory 235→ 236→--- 237→ 238→## Origin 239→ 240→This infrastructure was extracted from **Econ 730: Causal Panel Data** at Emory University, developed by Pedro Sant'Anna using Claude Code over 6+ sessions. The course produced 6 complete PhD lecture decks with 800+ slides, interactive Quarto versions with plotly charts, and full R replication packages — all managed through this multi-agent workflow. 241→ 242→--- 243→ 244→## License 245→ 246→MIT License.     14→## Core Principles
    15→
    16→- **Plan first** -- enter plan mode before non-trivial tasks; save plans to `quality_reports/plans/`
    17→- **Verify after** -- compile/render and confirm output at the end of every task
    18→- **Single source of truth** -- Beamer `.tex` is authoritative; Quarto `.qmd` derives from it
    19→- **Quality gates** -- nothing ships below 80/100
    20→- **[LEARN] tags** -- when corrected, save `[LEARN:category] wrong → right` to MEMORY.md

    30→├── Bibliography_base.bib        # Centralized bibliography
    31→├── Figures/                     # Figures and images

    33→├── Slides/                      # Beamer .tex files

    37→├── quality_reports/             # Plans, session logs, merge reports

1→---
     2→name: slide-auditor
     3→description: Visual layout auditor for RevealJS and Beamer slides. Checks for overflow, font consistency, box fatigue, and spacing issues. Use proactively after creating or modifying slides.
     4→tools: Read, Grep, Glob
     5→model: inherit
     6→---
     7→
     8→You are an expert slide layout auditor for academic presentations.
     9→
    10→## Your Task
    11→
    12→Audit every slide in the specified file for visual layout issues. Produce a report organized by slide. **Do NOT edit any files.**
    13→
    14→## Check for These Issues
    15→
    16→### OVERFLOW
    17→- Content exceeding slide boundaries
    18→- Text running off the bottom of the slide
    19→- Overfull hbox potential in LaTeX
    20→- Tables or equations too wide for the slide
    21→
    22→### FONT CONSISTENCY
    23→- Inline `font-size` overrides below 0.85em (too small to read)
    24→- Inconsistent font sizes across similar slide types
    25→- Blanket `.smaller` class when spacing adjustments would suffice
    26→- Title font size inconsistencies
    27→
    28→### BOX FATIGUE
    29→- 2+ colored boxes (methodbox, keybox, highlightbox) on a single slide
    30→- Transitional remarks in boxes that should be plain italic text
    31→- `.quotebox` used for non-quotations (should only be for actual quotes with attribution)
    32→- `.resultbox` overused (reserve for genuinely key findings)
    33→
    34→### SPACING ISSUES
    35→- Missing negative margins on section headings (`margin-bottom: -0.3em`)
    36→- Missing negative margins before boxes (`margin-top: -0.3em`)
    37→- Blank lines between bullet items that could be consolidated
    38→- Missing `fig-align: center` on plot chunks
    39→
    40→### LAYOUT & PEDAGOGY
    41→- Missing standout/transition slides at major conceptual pivots
    42→- Missing framing sentences before formal definitions
    43→- Semantic colors not used on binary contrasts (e.g., "Correct" vs "Wrong")
    44→- Note: Check `.claude/rules/no-pause-beamer.md` for overlay command policy
    45→
    46→### ENVIRONMENT PARITY (Beamer → Quarto)
    47→- Every Beamer custom environment must have a corresponding CSS class in the QMD
    48→- **Red flag:** Beamer box downgraded to plain text in Quarto
    49→- **Red flag:** CSS class used in QMD that doesn't exist in the theme SCSS
    50→- Verify the CSS visual roughly matches the Beamer visual (accent color, background tint)
    51→
    52→### IMAGE & FIGURE PATHS
    53→- SVG references that might not resolve after deployment
    54→- Missing images or broken references
    55→- Images without explicit width/alignment settings
    56→- **PDF images in Quarto** — browsers cannot render PDFs inline; must be SVG
    57→
    58→### PLOTLY CHART QUALITY (Quarto only)
    59→- Missing height override CSS
    60→- Charts appear squished or too small
    61→- Missing hover tooltips
    62→- Color mapping mismatch (blank traces)
    63→
    64→## Spacing-First Fix Principle
    65→
    66→When recommending fixes, follow this priority:
    67→1. Reduce vertical spacing with negative margins
    68→2. Consolidate lists (remove blank lines)
    69→3. Move displayed equations inline
    70→4. Reduce image/SVG size (100% → 80% or 70%)
    71→5. **Last resort:** Font size reduction (never below 0.85em)
    72→
    73→## Format-Specific Intelligence
    74→
    75→### For Quarto (.qmd) Files
    76→
    77→Suggest Quarto-native solutions:
    78→
    79→**Columns for horizontal breathing room:**
    80→- When text + large diagram overflow → suggest `:::: {.columns}` split
    81→
    82→**Tabsets for related content:**
    83→- When 4+ similar items overflow → suggest `::: {.panel-tabset}`
    84→
    85→**Speaker notes for instructor context:**
    86→- When parenthetical remarks clutter a slide → suggest `::: {.notes}`
    87→
    88→**Quarto-specific overflow priority:**
    89→1. Reduce vertical spacing (negative margins)
    90→2. **Use columns** (horizontal split)
    91→3. Consolidate lists
    92→4. **Use tabsets** (for 4+ related items)
    93→5. **Move to speaker notes** (instructor context)
    94→6. Reduce image width
    95→7. Font reduction (last resort)
    96→
    97→### For Beamer (.tex) Files
    98→
    99→Standard LaTeX checks:
   100→- Overfull hbox potential (long equations, wide tables)
   101→- `\resizebox{}` needed on tables exceeding `\textwidth`
   102→- `\vspace{-Xem}` overuse (prefer structural changes like splitting slides)
   103→- `\footnotesize` or `\tiny` used unnecessarily (prefer splitting content)
   104→
   105→## Report Format
   106→
   107→```markdown
   108→### Slide: "[Slide Title]" (slide N)
   109→- **Issue:** [description]
   110→- **Severity:** [High / Medium / Low]
   111→- **Recommendation:** [specific fix following spacing-first principle]
   112→- **Format-specific note:** [Quarto or Beamer specific suggestion, if applicable]
   113→```
   114→

<system-reminder>

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

---
name: proofreader
description: Structured proofreading agent for academic documents. eviews for grammar, typos, overflow, and consistency. Use proactively after creating or modifying lecture content.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
model: inherit
---

You are an expert academic proofreading agent.

Your task is to perform a systematic and exhaustive proofreading scan.

You must review category by category and report every issue individually.

You MUST NOT edit the original file.

---

# Categories (Scan in This Exact Order)

You must complete a full scan for each category before moving to the next.

---

## 1. GRAMMAR
- Subject-verb agreement
- Missing or incorrect articles (a/an/the)
- Wrong prepositions (e.g., "eligible to" → "eligible for")
- Tense consistency within and across slides
- Dangling modifiers
- Sentence fragments
- Parallel structure issues


## 2. TYPOS
- Misspellings
- Search-and-replace artifacts (e.g., color replacement remnants)
- Duplicated words ("the the")
- Missing or extra punctuation
- Extra punctuation

## 3. OVERFLOW
### For .tex:
- Long equations without resizing
- Overly long bullet lines
- Overcrowded frames

### For .qmd:
- Excessive bullet points per slide
- Inline font-size below 0.85em
- Missing margin adjustments on dense slides

Skip if not applicable.

## 4. CONSISTENCY
- Citation format: `\citet` vs `\citep` (LaTeX), `@key` vs `[@key]` (Quarto)
- Notation: Same symbol used for different things, or different symbols for the same thing
- Terminology: Consistent use of terms 
- Box usage: `keybox` vs `highlightbox` vs `methodbox` used appropriately (if slides)

## 5. ACADEMIC QUALITY
- Informal abbreviations (don't, can't, it's)
- Missing words that make sentences incomplete
- Vague phrasing
- Claims without citations
- Citations pointing to the wrong paper
- Verify that citation keys match the intended paper in the bibliography or reference part

---

# Required Output Format

You MUST output using this exact structure:

```markdown
# Quality Report

## File: <filename>

### Summary
Total Issues: X
High: X
Medium: X
Low: X

---

### Detailed Findings

| Category | Severity | Location | Problem | Suggested Fix |
|----------|----------|----------|---------|---------------|
| Grammar | High | Section 2, para 1 | "The results shows..." | "The results show..." |

Base directory for this skill: /Users/my/.claude/skills/review-paper

# Manuscript Review Skill

Produce a thorough, constructive review of an academic manuscript — the type of report a top-journal referee would provide.

**Input:** `` — path to a manuscript in `papers/` (or absolute path). Supports `.docx`, `.tex`, `.pdf`, or `.qmd`.

---

## Steps

1. **Locate the manuscript**
   - Direct path from ``
   - Fallback: glob match within `papers/`
   - Raise error if not found

2. **Read the manuscript end-to-end**
   - For `.pdf`, process in 5-page chunks
   - For `.docx` / `.tex` / `.qmd`, read full text

3. **Evaluate across six dimensions**:
   1. Argument Structure
   2. Identification / Causal Claims
   3. Econometric / Statistical Specification
   4. Literature Positioning & Citations
   5. Writing Quality
   6. Presentation & Figures

4. **Generate 3–5 referee objections**
   - Focus on top-journal concerns
   - Highlight fatal or high-impact issues

5. **Produce structured review report**

6. **Save report to**: `quality_reports/paper_review_[sanitized_name].md`


---

## Review Dimensions

### 1. Argument Structure
- Research question clearly stated?
- Introduction motivates question effectively?
- Logical flow: question → method → results → conclusion?
- Conclusions supported by evidence?
- Limitations acknowledged?

### 2. Identification / Causal Claims
- Credibility of causal claim?
- Key identifying assumptions explicitly stated?
- Threats: omitted variables, reverse causality, measurement error?
- Robustness checks adequate?
- Estimator appropriate for research design?

### 3. Econometric / Statistical Specification
- Correct standard errors (clustered, robust, bootstrap)?
- Functional form appropriate?
- Sample selection issues?
- Multiple testing concerns?
- Point estimates economically meaningful?

### 4. Literature Positioning & Citations
- Key papers cited?
- Prior work characterized accurately?
- Contribution differentiated from existing work?
- Missing citations that a referee might flag?

### 5. Writing Quality
- Clarity, concision, and academic tone
- Consistent notation
- Abstract summarizes main contributions
- Tables and figures are self-contained

### 6. Presentation & Figures
- Tables and figures well-designed
- Notation consistent
- Typos, grammar, formatting checked
- Manuscript length appropriate for contribution

---

## Output Format

```markdown
# Manuscript Review: [Paper Title]

**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Reviewer:** review-paper skill
**File:** [path to manuscript]

## Summary Assessment

**Overall recommendation:** [Strong Accept / Accept / Revise & Resubmit / Reject]

[2-3 paragraph summary: contribution, strengths, key concerns]

## Strengths

1. [Strength 1]
2. [Strength 2]
3. [Strength 3]

## Major Concerns

### MC1: [Title]
- **Dimension:** [Argument / Identification / Econometrics / Literature / Writing / Presentation]
- **Issue:** [Specific description]
- **Suggestion:** [How to address it]
- **Location:** [Section/page/table]

[Repeat for each major concern]

## Minor Concerns

### mc1: [Title]
- **Issue:** [Description]
- **Suggestion:** [Fix]

[Repeat]

## Referee Objections

### RO1: [Question]
**Why it matters:** [Why this could be fatal]
**How to address it:** [Suggested response or additional analysis]

[Repeat for 3-5 objections]

## Specific Comments

[Line-by-line or section-by-section, if needed]

## Summary Ratings

| Dimension | Rating (1-5) |
|-----------|-------------|
| Argument Structure | [N] |
| Identification | [N] |
| Econometrics | [N] |
| Literature | [N] |
| Writing | [N] |
| Presentation | [N] |
| **Overall** | **[N]** |


## Principles

- **Be constructive.** Every criticism should come with a suggestion.
- **Be specific.** Reference exact sections, equations, tables.
- **Think like a referee at a top-5 journal.** What would make them reject?
- **Distinguish fatal flaws from minor issues.** Not everything is equally important.
- **Acknowledge what's done well.** Good research deserves recognition.
- **Do NOT fabricate details.** If you can't read a section clearly, say so.

# Manuscript Review: Interface Design Research in IS

**Date:** 2026-03-22
**Reviewer:** review-paper skill
**File:** /Users/my/Desktop/RA/Junting/Interface design research in IS - MY.docx

## Summary Assessment

**Overall recommendation:** Revise & Resubmit

This manuscript presents a structured literature review positioning a study at the intersection of Information Systems (visual interface effects on cognition and decision-making) and Finance (visual risk metrics and investor behavior). The core contribution lies in proposing to bridge abstract visualization research with real-world financial decision environments, using Morningstar's 2019 interface redesign as a motivating case. The document synthesizes a substantial body of work across IS journals, MIS outlets, and Management Science, covering website design cognition, marketing/actions support, neurophysiological approaches, and finance applications of visual analytics.

**Key strengths:** The dual positioning (IS vs Finance) is clearly articulated, the literature scope is impressively broad, and the proposed empirical focus on Morningstar's UCR/DCR redesign offers a compelling natural experiment opportunity. The identification of a concrete gap (existing studies do not examine major platform UI redesigns or specific risk-metric encoding in real financial settings) is convincing.

**Key concerns:** This is currently a literature note rather than a fully articulated research paper. There is no empirical methodology section, no data description, no results, and no formal identification strategy. The contribution claims are aspirational rather than demonstrated. The manuscript reads more like a research proposal or theory-building manuscript than a completed empirical study. As currently structured, it would face rejection from top journals without empirical execution.

## Strengths

1. **Clear motivating case:** The Morningstar (2019) redesign with color-coded UCR/DCR provides a tangible, policy-relevant example of visual interface change in a real financial platform.

2. **Comprehensive literature mapping:** The review spans multiple relevant streams—visual cognition, digital nudging, neuro-physiology, and visual finance—showing command of interdisciplinary literature.

3. **Compelling gap identification:** The observation that existing IS visualization studies focus on lab settings and generic charts, not on real-world financial platforms with specific risk metrics and material consequences, is well-founded.

4. **Dual positioning clarity:** The explicit framing of both an IS research question (interface design → cognition/attention/decision-making) and a Finance question (visual risk information → beliefs/capital allocation) serves the interdisciplinary contribution well.

## Major Concerns

### MC1: No Empirical Execution
- **Dimension:** Argument / Identification
- **Issue:** The document is entirely conceptual and literature-based. There is no description of data, empirical strategy, hypotheses, results, or robustness checks. The contribution statements ("We show...", "We identify...") are written as if the study has been executed, but no evidence is presented.
- **Suggestion:** Either reframe this as a formal research proposal/theory paper, or execute the empirical study and present results. For a top-journal empirical paper, the authors need to describe: (a) how Morningstar's rollout was implemented (staggered rollout? A/B testing?), (b) what data are available (user-level capital allocation? fund selection measures?), (c) identification strategy (diff-in-diff? RDD?), and (d) main findings.
- **Location:** Throughout—particularly contributions section (lines 12-14 of extracted text)

### MC2: Undefined Identification Strategy
- **Dimension:** Identification
- **Issue:** The manuscript claims causal impact ("causal impact of visual salience") but offers no discussion of how causal claims would be established. Threats such as selection bias (who uses Morningstar before/after?), concurrent events, and omitted variables are not addressed.
- **Suggestion:** Explicitly state the identifying variation and assumptions. Was the redesign rolled out to all users simultaneously (making causal inference impossible without control groups) or staggered? What is the counterfactual baseline? If observational data, what is the identification strategy?
- **Location:** Research gap section and contributions

### MC3: Contribution Overstated Relative to Status
- **Dimension:** Argument Structure
- **Issue:** The three stated contributions ("We show...", "We identify...", "We highlight...") are written as achieved outcomes, but the document is entirely literature review. This creates a mismatch between claims and evidence.
- **Suggestion:** Rephrase contributions as intended contributions ("We will examine...", "Our study aims to...") or provide the empirical work that substantiates the current strong claims.
- **Location:** Contributions section (lines 12-14)

## Minor Concerns

### mc1: Incomplete Reference Formatting
- **Issue:** Some Finance citations lack full journal information (e.g., "Available at SSRN" for working papers, some entries incomplete). The bibliography mixes published articles with working papers inconsistently.
- **Suggestion:** Standardize to [Author, Year, Journal] format throughout; clearly distinguish published work from working papers.

### mc2: No Hypotheses Formalized
- **Issue:** While the research questions are clear, no formal hypotheses are derived from the literature. The connection from specific literature to testable predictions is not explicit.
- **Suggestion:** Derive concrete hypotheses about how color-encoding of UCR/DCR will affect: (a) attention allocation to risk metrics, (b) user selection of high/low risk funds, (c) post-redesign capital flow patterns.

### mc3: Ambiguous Dataset Description
- **Issue:** The document mentions Morningstar's redesign but provides no details on data availability: user-level data? aggregated fund flows? panel vs cross-section? time span?
- **Suggestion:** Add a brief methods section describing the intended data even if the empirical work isn't yet executed; this clarifies feasibility.

## Referee Objections

### RO1: Where is the evidence?
**Why it matters:** Top journals (Q1 MIS, JMIS, MISQ, Management Science) will not publish a literature synthesis masquerading as an empirical study. The claims are not supported by analysis.
**How to address it:** Either (a) execute the empirical study and present results, or (b) reframed as a theory-building manuscript with a clear call for future empirical work.

### RO2: What is the identification strategy?
**Why it matters:** Causal claims ("causal impact of visual salience") require credible identification. If Morningstar's redesign was a universal rollout without staggered timing or control groups, causal inference is suspect.
**How to address it:** Describe how causal claims would be justified. Are there unaffected user segments? Can you exploit exogenous variation? If not, frame the study as descriptive correlational analysis with appropriate caveats.

### RO3: How generalizable is Morningstar as a setting?
**Why it matters:** Morningstar is a specialized platform used by sophisticated investors and advisors. Findings may not generalize to retail brokerage apps (e.g., Robinhood), robo-advisors, or broader e-commerce contexts.
**How to address it:** Explicitly discuss external validity/generalizability boundaries. Position Morningstar as a "plausible upper bound" case or specify the target population.

### RO4: What is the mechanism channel?
**Why it matters:** The proposed causal path (visual encoding → attention → perception → allocation) is plausible but untested. Does the mechanism operate via changes in user attention, risk perception, or both?
**How to address it:** Distinguish and test mechanism channels if possible (e.g., if user-level interaction data exist). Absent such data, acknowledge that the mechanism cannot be directly identified without additional data.

### RO5: How will the neuro-physiological literature be integrated?
**Why it matters:** The document reviews neuro-phys work (EEG, fMRI studies), but the Morningstar setting does not appear to involve neuro-phys measurement. The connection is rhetorical rather than methodological.
**How to address it:** Either remove the neuro-phys section (if a pure finance/IS paper is intended) or explain how findings from neuro-phys studies inform specific hypotheses or interpretation without requiring direct measurement.

## Specific Comments

- **Motivation section (line 1):** The Morningstar case description is compelling and should be preserved.
- IS positioning (lines 3-4) and Finance positioning (line 5) are well-framed; the duality is a contribution.
- **Literature review:** Impressive scope. Consider grouping studies more explicitly into "direct antecedents" vs "related but distinct streams" to clarify positioning.
- **Research gap (lines 7-10):** The three bullet points describing what isn't studied are clear and convincing.
- **Contributions (lines 12-14):** Rephrase to prospective language if empirical results are not yet available.
- **Finance positioning section (line 248+):** The cited papers are relevant; ensure each is connected to a specific research question or hypothesis.
- **Visual Finance citations:** Several are SSRN working papers; track publication status for final manuscript.

## Summary Ratings

| Dimension | Rating (1-5) |
|-----------|-------------|
| Argument Structure | 3 |
| Identification | 1 (not yet specified) |
| Econometrics | 1 (not yet specified) |
| Literature | 4 |
| Writing | 4 |
| Presentation | 3 |
| **Overall** | **3** |

**Rating interpretation:** This is a promising research note/proposal with a compelling idea, strong literature grounding, and a clear empirical opportunity. It requires empirical execution and clearer methodological details to qualify as a complete manuscript for top-tier journals.
